quote: But, yeah, unfortunately, as of right now, it's not really gonna make sense for you guys, mainly because, you're not gonna see as much savings.
stewart_read: The rep gives an incorrect rationale for the DQ. The actual disqualifier is the credit threshold, not a savings calculation. Saying 'you're not gonna see as much savings' is not the DQ logic — it's a fabricated softener that misrepresents why the call is ending. The transcript supports that the rep delivered incorrect rationale; whether the rep fully understands the underlying DQ logic is a separate question the manager should probe in the 1-on-1.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep softened the exit deliberately to preserve goodwill for the follow-up call. Rejected — the softener uses inaccurate framing that could create confusion or distrust when the customer later learns the real reason.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk the rep through the actual DQ logic: the disqualifier is the credit threshold, not a savings projection. The rep needs to be able to state the real reason cleanly. Separately, the codex's unresolved policy question (should setters DQ on stated credit at all?) should be resolved with Kenny before this rep is coached further on credit handling.

quote: Okay. Is that gonna be an issue for both of you or just one of you?
stewart_read: This is the one moment where the rep shows investigative instinct — asking whether the credit issue applies to both owners or just one. It's the right question. But the rep then accepts John's answer ('it's a issue for me / Michael has no credit at all') and exits without distinguishing between 'bad credit' and 'no credit history.' No credit history is not the same as a sub-670 score, and the rep doesn't probe further or route to a closer for the actual pull.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: the rep asked the right question but didn't know what to do with the answer. Strategic read: the rep was setting up a co-applicant path. Reactive read holds — the follow-through is absent.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show the rep they had the right instinct and then stopped one step short. The coaching question: 'What's the difference between no credit and bad credit, and what do we do with each?' Route to closer for the actual pull rather than self-adjudicating on stated scores.

stewart_read: John gives a clean bill number. The rep's acknowledgment is garbled — 'About one point. Okay.' — and the bill is immediately dropped. No flip, no emotional alignment against the utility, no 'what would it mean to you if that went to zero?' The bill is captured as data and abandoned. The garbled acknowledgment ('About one point') also suggests the rep may not have registered the number correctly, which compounds the miss.
coaching_implication: Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. The rep did neither the flip nor a clean acknowledgment. In the 1-on-1, replay this moment and ask: 'What could you have said here that would have made John feel the pain of that $120 before you moved to credit?' No fabricated numbers needed — the pain the number represents is the leverage.

quote: looks like I have you down as the property owner. Is there anybody else you can paddle with you, or is it just you?
stewart_read: The rep is executing the verify.title_co_owners step proactively — this is correct procedure. The phrasing is garbled ('anybody else you can paddle with you' is likely a transcription artifact of 'anybody else on the title with you'), but the intent is right. John surfaces Michael Hayes here, which is the co-owner the rep later uses in the credit probe. The verify step is doing its job.
coaching_implication: No failure here — flag as a positive verify execution. The co-owner data collected at 01:35 directly enabled the credit probe at 02:27. Use this as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1 before moving to the DQ handling failure.

quote: Good to hear. I'll keep it short for you. What got you interested in looking at solar?
stewart_read: The rep opens with a speed-framing ('I'll keep it short') before John has signaled any time pressure. This is an opener that pre-emptively shrinks the call's value ceiling — it signals to John that this will be transactional, not consultative. The interest question that follows ('What got you interested in looking at solar?') is the right question per verify.interest_in_solar, but the setup undercuts it. John's shallow answer ('Clean energy, I guess. I don't know.') is at least partly a product of the low-energy frame the rep established.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep read John's brief early responses as low-engagement and matched energy to avoid friction. Reactive read holds — the speed-framing came before John gave any signal of impatience, so it was a default posture, not a read.
coaching_implication: Per coaching_philosophy.energy_match, the opener sets the energy ceiling for the call. 'I'll keep it short' is a ceiling-lowering move. Manager should ask the rep: 'What did John tell you in the first 10 seconds that made you think he needed it short?' If the answer is nothing, that's the coaching point — don't pre-shrink the call.

quote: Very very beautiful home. Looks like you get lots of months of sunlight, so so it shouldn't be an issue at all.
stewart_read: The rep uses satellite imagery to compliment the home and signal preparation — a small trust-building move that personalizes the call. Per coaching_philosophy.energy_match, this is the kind of neighbor-like, knowledgeable energy that converts with the 45-70 demographic. It's a brief positive moment in an otherwise procedural call.
coaching_implication: Flag as a positive micro-trade. The rep knows how to use the satellite view for rapport. The coaching question is why this energy didn't carry forward into the bill anchor or the credit conversation.

quote: Clean energy, I guess. I don't know.
stewart_read: This is a weak enthusiasm signal — John names a motive (clean energy) but hedges immediately. Per verify.interest_in_solar, depth matters more than the answer, and top setters spend 60+ seconds here. The rep acknowledges ('No. That makes sense') and moves on in under 5 seconds. The motive is checked, not explored. There is no follow-up that could have surfaced a stronger emotional anchor — environmental conviction, neighbor influence, or a specific bill event.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep read the hedge as a signal that clean energy wasn't a real motivator and pivoted to bill pain instead. Rejected — the rep never pivoted to bill pain as an emotional anchor either. The interest question was checked and dropped.
coaching_implication: Per verify.interest_in_solar, the rep needed at least one follow-up here: 'What's driving that for you — is it more the environmental side or the cost side?' Even a shallow answer gives the rep an anchor to return to later. Manager should replay this moment and ask: 'What would you have done with 60 seconds here?'

quote: I have Michael has no credit at all because he because he's never used credit in that.
stewart_read: John draws a clear distinction: his own credit is the issue, and Michael has no credit history — not bad credit. The rep treats both as equivalent and exits. 'No credit history' is a materially different situation from a sub-670 score, and the rep's failure to distinguish between them suggests either a knowledge gap on what 'no credit' means for qualification, or an absence of any escalation path for edge cases. The codex's unresolved policy question (should setters DQ on stated credit?) is directly relevant here.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep correctly identified that neither applicant could qualify and exited efficiently. Rejected — the rep never verified John's stated score or explored whether 'no credit history' could be handled differently at the closer stage.
coaching_implication: This is the moment where the call needed a closer, not a setter decision. Manager should establish a clear protocol: when a customer says 'no credit history' (as opposed to 'bad credit'), what is the rep supposed to do? Route to closer for the actual pull, or DQ on stated? Until Kenny resolves the policy question in qualifiers.credit_qualifier, this rep will keep making the same exit.
